title: HardInterventionOn
lean_code_with_comments:
def hardInterventionOn (G : CDMG Node) (W : Finset Node)
    (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) : CDMG Node where
  J := G.J ∪ W
  V := G.V \ W
  hJV_disj := by
    -- `hW` is part of the signature for LN-faithfulness ("Let
    -- `W ⊆ J ∪ V`"), but is not consumed in the five proof obligations:
    -- `def_3_1`'s typing constraints on `G.E` / `G.L` already exclude
    -- every problematic case (see the `W ∩ J ≠ ∅` design-choice bullet
    -- above).  The `let _` mirrors the convention used in
    -- `CDMGRestrictions.lean` for unused LN-faithful hypotheses.
    let _ := hW
    refine Finset.disjoint_union_left.mpr ⟨?_, ?_⟩
    · exact Finset.disjoint_left.mpr fun a haJ haVW =>
        Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj haJ (Finset.mem_sdiff.mp haVW).1
    · exact Finset.disjoint_left.mpr fun a haW haVW =>
        (Finset.mem_sdiff.mp haVW).2 haW
  E := G.E.filter (fun e => e.2 ∉ W)
  hE_subset := by
    intro e he
    obtain ⟨heE, he2⟩ := Finset.mem_filter.mp he
    obtain ⟨he1, he2V⟩ := G.hE_subset heE
    refine ⟨?_, Finset.mem_sdiff.mpr ⟨he2V, he2⟩⟩
    rcases Finset.mem_union.mp he1 with hJ | hV
    · exact Finset.mem_union_left _ (Finset.mem_union_left _ hJ)
    · by_cases hW1 : e.1 ∈ W
      · exact Finset.mem_union_left _ (Finset.mem_union_right _ hW1)
      · exact Finset.mem_union_right _ (Finset.mem_sdiff.mpr ⟨hV, hW1⟩)
  L := G.L.filter (fun e => e.1 ∉ W ∧ e.2 ∉ W)
  hL_subset := by
    intro e he
    obtain ⟨heL, he1, he2⟩ := Finset.mem_filter.mp he
    obtain ⟨he1V, he2V⟩ := G.hL_subset heL
    exact ⟨Finset.mem_sdiff.mpr ⟨he1V, he1⟩, Finset.mem_sdiff.mpr ⟨he2V, he2⟩⟩
  hL_irrefl := by
    intro v1 v2 h
    exact G.hL_irrefl (Finset.mem_filter.mp h).1
  hL_symm := by
    intro v1 v2 h
    obtain ⟨hL, h1, h2⟩ := Finset.mem_filter.mp h
    exact Finset.mem_filter.mpr ⟨G.hL_symm hL, h2, h1⟩
lean_code_without_comments:
def hardInterventionOn (G : CDMG Node) (W : Finset Node)
    (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) : CDMG Node where
  J := G.J ∪ W
  V := G.V \ W
  hJV_disj := by

    let _ := hW
    refine Finset.disjoint_union_left.mpr ⟨?_, ?_⟩
    · exact Finset.disjoint_left.mpr fun a haJ haVW =>
        Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj haJ (Finset.mem_sdiff.mp haVW).1
    · exact Finset.disjoint_left.mpr fun a haW haVW =>
        (Finset.mem_sdiff.mp haVW).2 haW
  E := G.E.filter (fun e => e.2 ∉ W)
  hE_subset := by
    intro e he
    obtain ⟨heE, he2⟩ := Finset.mem_filter.mp he
    obtain ⟨he1, he2V⟩ := G.hE_subset heE
    refine ⟨?_, Finset.mem_sdiff.mpr ⟨he2V, he2⟩⟩
    rcases Finset.mem_union.mp he1 with hJ | hV
    · exact Finset.mem_union_left _ (Finset.mem_union_left _ hJ)
    · by_cases hW1 : e.1 ∈ W
      · exact Finset.mem_union_left _ (Finset.mem_union_right _ hW1)
      · exact Finset.mem_union_right _ (Finset.mem_sdiff.mpr ⟨hV, hW1⟩)
  L := G.L.filter (fun e => e.1 ∉ W ∧ e.2 ∉ W)
  hL_subset := by
    intro e he
    obtain ⟨heL, he1, he2⟩ := Finset.mem_filter.mp he
    obtain ⟨he1V, he2V⟩ := G.hL_subset heL
    exact ⟨Finset.mem_sdiff.mpr ⟨he1V, he1⟩, Finset.mem_sdiff.mpr ⟨he2V, he2⟩⟩
  hL_irrefl := by
    intro v1 v2 h
    exact G.hL_irrefl (Finset.mem_filter.mp h).1
  hL_symm := by
    intro v1 v2 h
    obtain ⟨hL, h1, h2⟩ := Finset.mem_filter.mp h
    exact Finset.mem_filter.mpr ⟨G.hL_symm hL, h2, h1⟩
lean_explanation:
The Lean declaration `hardInterventionOn` is a function `CDMG Node → Finset Node → … → CDMG Node` that, given a CDMG `G`, a subset `W` of its node set, and a witness `hW` that `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`, returns the intervened CDMG $G_{\doit(W)}$. The binder `Node : Type*` (inherited from the file-level `variable` scope, together with the typeclass `[DecidableEq Node]`) is the ambient vertex carrier — the analogue of the LN's silent assumption that all node sets live in a common universe. `DecidableEq Node` is the mild constraint Lean needs to evaluate the membership predicates `e.2 ∉ W` and `e.1 ∉ W ∧ e.2 ∉ W` inside `Finset.filter`. The intervention set is `W : Finset Node`, and the LN's "Let $W \subseteq J \cup V$" is recorded as the explicit argument `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`.

The body uses Lean's `where`-syntax to construct the output `CDMG Node` field by field, mirroring the four data fields and five constraint fields of the `def_3_1` structure. Two Mathlib idioms warrant mention. `Finset.filter` is the Lean primitive matching the LN's set-builder notation: a fact `e ∈ s.filter p` rewrites via `Finset.mem_filter` to `e ∈ s ∧ p e`, which is exactly the elementhood version of $\{ e \in s \mid p\,e \}$. The LN's set-difference symbol becomes the `Finset` operator `\`, and `Finset.mem_sdiff` unpacks `e ∈ A \ B` as `e ∈ A ∧ e ∉ B`. The disjointness obligation for the new $J$ and $V$ is discharged through `Finset.disjoint_union_left`, splitting the goal `(G.J ∪ W) ∩ (G.V \ W) = ∅` into the two natural sub-claims.

The constructor populates all nine fields of the output CDMG: the four data assignments (`J := G.J ∪ W`, `V := G.V \ W`, `E := G.E.filter (fun e => e.2 ∉ W)`, `L := G.L.filter (fun e => e.1 ∉ W ∧ e.2 ∉ W)`) and five proof obligations (`hJV_disj`, `hE_subset`, `hL_subset`, `hL_irrefl`, `hL_symm`), each discharged in a few lines by combining `G`'s own axioms with the structural fact that filtering shrinks a set. Notably none of the proofs consume `hW`: the typing constraints `G.E ⊆ (G.J ∪ G.V) × G.V` and `G.L ⊆ G.V × G.V` carried by every CDMG already supply the membership facts the obligations need, so the precondition is present purely for LN-faithfulness of the signature.
design_choices:
**The output is a plain `def`, not a wrapping `structure` or `inductive`.** A hard intervention is a function `CDMG Node → Finset Node → … → CDMG Node`, producing a new CDMG from an existing one. Wrapping the result in a fresh record carrying the intervened graph as a field would force every downstream consumer — the do-calculus chapters, the intervention algebra of structural causal models — to re-destructure the same fields they already pattern-match on for ordinary CDMGs. An `inductive` was rejected for the same reason: pattern-matching would obscure the LN's direct naming of the four components.

**The precondition `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` is an explicit argument, not an instance or implicit.** Subset facts are not typeclass-resolvable in any general way, so pushing them into an `instance` would force every caller to discharge the membership manually. Keeping `hW` explicit preserves the LN-faithful precondition at every call site, even though none of the five proof obligations on the output CDMG actually consume it — `def_3_1`'s typing axioms already discharge them.

**Edge removals use `Finset.filter`.** The LN writes the removed sets in set-builder form $E \setminus \{\ldots\}$; `Finset.filter` is the closest Lean primitive, and `Finset.mem_filter` matches the LN's elementhood reading directly. `Finset.image` was rejected because it re-maps elements rather than takes a difference; a `Sym2`-quotient encoding of bidirected edges was rejected back at `def_3_1` in favour of the ordered-pair-plus-symmetry shape, and that decision is inherited here.

**Directed-edge removal is one-sided (`e.2 ∉ W`); bidirected-edge removal is two-sided (`e.1 ∉ W ∧ e.2 ∉ W`).** This is the load-bearing encoding decision and a registered content deviation from the LN's literal item iv. Under the ordered-pair encoding of `L` with the symmetry axiom $(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$, a literal one-sided filter would fail to preserve symmetry: for $w \in W \cap V$ and $v \in V \setminus W$, the mirror pairs $(v, w)$ and $(w, v)$ both encode the same bidirected edge between $v$ and $w$, but the one-sided rule deletes only the first. The resulting four-tuple would then fail the `hL_symm` axiom and would not be a CDMG at all. The two-sided filter is the unique reading consistent both with the LN's natural-language gloss "remove all edges into nodes from $W$" — a bidirected edge is *into both* of its endpoints — and with the LN's own assertion that $G_{\doit(W)}$ is itself a CDMG. Concretely, on the two-node graph with $L = \{(0,1), (1,0)\}$ and $W = \{1\}$, the one-sided filter yields the non-symmetric $\{(1, 0)\}$; the two-sided filter yields the only legal answer, $\emptyset$.

**The case $W \cap J \neq \emptyset$ is admitted, not forbidden.** The LN hypothesis is $W \subseteq J \cup V$, with no additional $W \cap J = \emptyset$ clause. When $W$ overlaps the input set, items i and ii are idempotent on $W \cap J$ ($J \cup W$ is unchanged at those nodes, and $V \setminus W$ is unaffected because $J$ and $V$ are disjoint), and items iii and iv are de facto no-ops because the CDMG typing constraints forbid any edge from having its head in $J$. Idempotency is not baked into the return type; downstream lemmas derive it as a corollary when needed.

**The output is built with `where`-syntax and named fields, not the anonymous constructor `⟨ … ⟩`.** The CDMG structure has nine fields, four of data and five of proof. An anonymous positional list would interleave them and obscure the correspondence with `def_3_1`; named-field syntax keeps each obligation labelled and adjacent to the data it constrains.
